success_surface: Formula-based character extraction across target cell range with verification
effective_behaviors: Conducted pre-implementation inspection of spreadsheet structure before creating solutions
Generated formulas dynamically based on cell position rather than hardcoding values
Targeted only the specified answer_position range without overextending modifications
Used openpyxl to write formulas as raw string values for proper Excel interpretation
Performed post-save verification by reloading the output file to confirm formula integrity
Preserved the original source file by saving to a separate output path
evidence: Agent executed initial exploration code examining Sheet1 Structure, max_row, max_column, and existing formulas before implementing solution
Formulas used MID function with computed char_position based on column number: f'=MID(A{row_num},{char_position},1)' instead of hardcoded character values
Loop iterated exactly over rows 5-7 and columns 4-11 matching answer_position D5:K7 specification
Cell assignments used target_cell.value = formula format to insert raw formula strings like '=MID(A5,1,1)'
Verification block reloaded wb_verify = load_workbook(output_path) and printed all formulas in the target range after saving
Output was written to output_path '/mnt/data/outputs/.../1_59639_output.xlsx' without modifying the original init.xlsx file

memory_items.title: Exploration Before Implementation
memory_items.lesson: Always examine spreadsheet structure (dimensions, existing content, formulas) before writing modification code
memory_items.generalization: Reduce errors by programmatically querying sheet properties, cell values, and existing formulas before generating solutions. This informs range boundaries and existing data patterns.

memory_items.title: Dynamic Formula Construction
memory_items.lesson: Create Excel formulas programmatically using position variables instead of static values when manipulating multiple cells
memory_items.generalization: For batch operations requiring similar logic across cells, construct formula strings with loop variables (row_num, col_num, position_offset) to maintain consistency and enable easy adjustment.

memory_items.title: Precise Range Targeting
memory_items.lesson: Iterate only within the explicitly specified answer_position bounds
memory_items.generalization: When given a target cell range constraint, calculate start/end indices precisely and iterate exclusively within those bounds to avoid unintended modifications outside scope.

memory_items.title: Formula Insertion Method
memory_items.lesson: Use openpyxl cell.value to write formulas as raw strings starting with '='
memory_items.generalization: When inserting formulas via openpyxl, assign them as string literals prefixed with '='. Do not evaluate formulas manually; let Excel handle calculation after file opening.

memory_items.title: Post-Save Validation
memory_items.lesson: Reload the saved workbook to verify formulas were correctly persisted
memory_items.generalization: After saving, reload the output file programmatically and inspect critical cells to confirm formulas retained their syntax and references before reporting success.

edits.content:
## 6. Trace-Derived Lessons

### Exploration Before Implementation
- Always examine spreadsheet structure (max rows, columns, existing content) before generating modification code.
- Programmatically query sheet properties and sample cells to inform range boundaries and detect existing formulas.
- This prevents errors from assumptions about data layout and helps identify reference patterns.

### Dynamic Formula Construction
- For batch operations requiring similar logic across cells, construct formula strings with loop variables (row_num, col_num, position_offset).
- Use functions like MID, INDEX, OFFSET with calculated parameters instead of hardcoding static values.
- Maintain consistent offset calculations based on target range dimensions (e.g., char_position = col_num - start_col + 1).

### Precise Range Targeting
- When given explicit answer_position constraints, calculate exact start/end row and column indices.
- Iterate exclusively within these bounds using inclusive/exclusive ranges appropriately.
- Avoid over-extending loops beyond specified target areas to prevent unintended modifications.

### Formula Insertion Method
- Use openpyxl cell.value = "=FORMULA(...)" to insert formulas as raw strings.
- Ensure formulas start with '=' and use proper Excel syntax.
- Let Excel handle evaluation when the file is opened; do not attempt to pre-evaluate formulas in Python.

### Post-Save Validation
- After saving, reload the output workbook and inspect target cells to verify formulas persisted correctly.
- Check both formula string content and expected cell values where applicable.
- Include verification output in logs to confirm successful formula insertion before reporting completion.
